architecture.summary: Logic-LM is a neuro-symbolic framework that decomposes logical reasoning into three stages: (1) Problem Formulation, where an LLM translates a natural language problem into symbolic representations (logic programming, first-order logic, constraint satisfaction, or SAT formulas) via few-shot in-context learning; (2) Symbolic Reasoning, where a deterministic external solver (Pyke, Prover9, python-constraint, or Z3) performs inference on the formulated problem; (3) Result Interpretation, where outputs are mapped back to natural language answers. A self-refinement module iteratively revises symbolic formalizations using error messages from the solver as feedback.

biblio.title: Logic-LM: Empowering Large Language Models with Symbolic Solvers for Faithful Logical Reasoning

claims.dataset: five logical reasoning datasets (ProofWriter, PrOntoQA, FOLIO, LogicalDeduction, AR-LSAT)
claims.statement: Logic-LM achieves an average performance boost of 39.2% over using LLM alone with standard prompting

claims.dataset: five logical reasoning datasets (ProofWriter, PrOntoQA, FOLIO, LogicalDeduction, AR-LSAT)
claims.statement: Logic-LM achieves an average improvement of 18.4% over LLM with chain-of-thought prompting
With GPT-3.5, Logic-LM outperforms standard LLM on all datasets with an average improvement of 39.2%
GPT-4 achieves executable rate (Exe_Rate) of 79.9% on FOLIO and 32.6% on AR-LSAT
On ProofWriter depth-5 problems, Logic-LM outperforms CoT by 39.4%
Self-refinement increases executable rate by 5.01 percentage points on average

GPT-4 demonstrates near 100% executable rates on synthetic datasets (PrOntoQA, ProofWriter, LogicalDeduction) but notably lower performance on real-world expert-written datasets (FOLIO and AR-LSAT), with Exe_Rate of 79.9% and 32.6% respectively

Self-refinement increases executable formulations but accuracy tends to stagnate in subsequent rounds because error messages from the symbolic solver fix grammar validity but not semantic correctness of problem formulation
